name: Zoroaster
deeds: Joyous Laughter: Instead of crying at birth, he laughed - the only infant in history to do so
Heavenly Light: A radiant glow surrounded him
Protected by Divine Forces: Attempts by evil priests to kill the infant were thwarted
Fravashi's Glory: His divine spirit (fravashi) was visible to the spiritually aware
The worship of daevas (demons) as gods
Oppression of the weak by warrior clans
Lack of ethical foundation in religious practice
A shining figure appeared - Vohu Manah (Good Mind), the first Amesha Spenta
description:
The founding prophet of Zoroastrianism, who received divine revelation from Ahura Mazda and established the religion of truth. Zoroaster (Avestan: Zarathustra; Persian: Zartosht) is revered as humanity's greatest teacher, the one who revealed the cosmic conflict between Asha and Druj, and taught the Threefold Path of righteousness.

Zoroaster was born in northeastern Iran (possibly Airyana Vaejah, the legendary Aryan homeland). His birth was accompanied by miraculous signs:

Zoroaster served as a priest (zaotar) in the old polytheistic religion but became increasingly troubled by:

keyMyths.title: The Divine Revelation on Mount Ushidar
keyMyths.description: Zoroaster receives his first divine revelation from Ahura Mazda on Mount Ushidar, where he is instructed to preach the truth and combat the forces of Angra Mainyu. This encounter marks the beginning of his prophetic mission and the establishment of Zoroastrianism.

keyMyths.title: The Temptation by Angra Mainyu
keyMyths.description: Angra Mainyu, the destructive spirit, attempts to dissuade Zoroaster from his path by offering him worldly power and dominion. Zoroaster steadfastly rejects these temptations, affirming his commitment to Ahura Mazda and the path of righteousness (Asha).

keyMyths.title: Conversion of King Vishtaspa
keyMyths.description: After facing initial resistance and persecution, Zoroaster eventually converts King Vishtaspa to Zoroastrianism. This conversion provides Zoroaster with royal patronage and enables the spread of his teachings throughout the kingdom, establishing a Zoroastrian society.

keyMyths.title: The Trial by Fire
keyMyths.description: To prove the truth of his message and the falseness of his accusers, Zoroaster undergoes a trial by fire. He emerges unharmed, demonstrating the divine protection of Ahura Mazda and silencing his opponents.
keyMyths.source: Shahnameh (though a later source, it reflects the tradition)

keyMyths.title: The Cosmic Battle
keyMyths.description: Zoroaster actively participates in the cosmic battle against the forces of Druj (falsehood) through his prayers, teachings, and righteous actions. He serves as a champion of Asha, working to purify the world and prepare it for the final renovation (Frashokereti).

achievements: Received divine revelation from Ahura Mazda
Established Zoroastrianism, a monotheistic religion emphasizing good thoughts, good words, and good deeds.
Converted King Vishtaspa and secured royal patronage for the faith
Overcame the temptations of Angra Mainyu
Successfully underwent a trial by fire, proving his divine connection
Developed a complex cosmology and eschatology centered on the cosmic battle between good and evil
Promulgated the concept of free will and individual responsibility for one's actions
Introduced the concept of a final judgment and resurrection
Established a moral framework based on the Threefold Path of righteousness (Humata, Hukhta, Huvarsta)

sources.name: Avesta
sources.description: The primary collection of Zoroastrian religious texts, including the Gathas (hymns attributed to Zoroaster himself).
sources.title: Avesta

sources.name: Yasna
sources.description: A liturgical collection within the Avesta, containing hymns and prayers recited during Zoroastrian rituals.
sources.title: Yasna

sources.name: Vendidad
sources.description: A section of the Avesta dealing with purification rituals, myths, and laws.
sources.title: Vendidad

sources.name: Denkard
sources.description: A 9th-century AD compilation of Zoroastrian beliefs and practices.
sources.title: Denkard

sources.name: Shahnameh
sources.description: The Persian 'Book of Kings' by Ferdowsi, contains legendary accounts of Zoroaster's life and times, though it's a much later and less reliable source for early Zoroastrian history.
sources.title: Shahnameh
